_note: Curated reference vocabulary & grammar for the Learning Hub dictionary. Merged with lesson-derived terms at runtime (see buildDictionary in learn.js). Each vocab group: {category, level, words:[[ko,en],...]}. Each grammar group: {category, level, patterns:[[pattern,meaning],...]}.

words: thank you for your hard work

patterns.form: Add -습니다 to a stem ending in a consonant, -ㅂ니다 to a stem ending in a vowel (먹다 → 먹습니다, 가다 → 갑니다).
patterns.exp: This is the most formal, polite way to make a statement. You'll see it everywhere in TOPIK reading passages, announcements, and news. It sounds respectful and a little stiff, which is exactly why it's used in writing and formal speech.

patterns.form: The question form of -습니다: -습니까? after a consonant, -ㅂ니까? after a vowel.
patterns.exp: Use this to ask a question politely and formally. It is simply the question version of -습니다 — the meaning is the same, only the sentence becomes a question.

patterns.form: Add -아요 if the last stem vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, otherwise -어요 (하다 verbs become -해요).
patterns.exp: This is the everyday polite ending used in conversation, messages, and friendly writing. It is polite but warmer and less stiff than -습니다. The same form works for statements, questions, and suggestions — only the intonation changes.

patterns.form: Insert -았- (after ㅏ/ㅗ) or -었- (otherwise) before the ending: 가다 → 갔어요, 먹다 → 먹었어요.
patterns.exp: This marks that something already happened. It goes right before the polite ending. Once you can spot -았/었-, you can instantly tell a sentence is about the past.

patterns.form: Add -을 거예요 after a consonant, -ㄹ 거예요 after a vowel.
patterns.exp: Use this to talk about the future or to guess about something. With 'I' it usually means a plan ('I will ~'); with other people it often means a guess ('probably ~').
ex: I will meet a friend tomorrow.

patterns.form: Attach -지 않다 to the verb/adjective stem: 가다 → 가지 않아요.
patterns.exp: This makes a sentence negative. It has the same meaning as putting 안 before the verb, but this 'long form' sounds a little more formal and is common in writing.

patterns.form: Simply put 안 right before the verb or adjective: 안 가요, 안 비싸요. For 하다 verbs, split them: 공부 안 해요.
patterns.exp: This is the quick, everyday way to make something negative. It means the same as -지 않다 but is shorter and more common in speech.

patterns.form: Put 못 before the verb: 못 가요, 못 먹어요. For 하다 verbs: 운동 못 해요.
patterns.exp: This says you are unable to do something — because of a lack of ability, permission, or circumstances. It is different from 안 (which means you simply don't/won't): 못 means you can't even if you wanted to.
ex: It's too spicy, so I can't eat it.
I was busy, so I couldn't go.

patterns.form: Add -으세요 after a consonant, -세요 after a vowel: 앉다 → 앉으세요, 가다 → 가세요.
patterns.exp: Use this to politely tell or ask someone to do something. It is friendly and respectful — the normal way to give instructions to customers, guests, or anyone you're being polite to.

patterns.form: Attach -고 있다 to the verb stem: 읽다 → 읽고 있어요.
patterns.exp: This says an action is happening right now, in progress. It matches the English '-ing'. Use it for actions you're in the middle of.
ex: I'm reading a book right now.

patterns.form: Attach -고 to the first verb/adjective stem: 먹다 → 먹고.
patterns.exp: Use -고 to join two actions or facts in one sentence. It simply lists them, often in time order ('do A and then B'). It does not carry any cause or contrast — just 'and'.
ex: I eat breakfast and then go to school.
These clothes are cheap and pretty.

patterns.form: Same vowel rule as -아요/어요: 아프다 → 아파서, 먹다 → 먹어서.
patterns.exp: This links two clauses where the first is the reason or the first step leading into the second. It's one of the most common ways to say 'because' in Korean. The two clauses are closely connected in meaning.
ex: My stomach hurt, so I went to the hospital.
I had no time, so I took a taxi.
patterns.note: Never add a past-tense -았/었- before -아서/어서. Say 아파서, not 아팠어서.

patterns.form: Attach -지만 to the stem: 비싸다 → 비싸지만.
patterns.exp: Use -지만 to contrast two ideas inside one sentence — the second clause goes against what you'd expect from the first. It's the basic word for 'but'.
ex: I studied hard, but I did poorly on the exam.

patterns.form: Add -으면 after a consonant, -면 after a vowel: 있다 → 있으면, 가다 → 가면.
patterns.exp: This sets up a condition: 'if/when the first thing happens, then the second.' It's used both for real conditions and for things that happen regularly.
ex: If you have time, let's meet.
When spring comes, the flowers bloom.

patterns.form: Add -으러 after a consonant, -러 after a vowel, then a movement verb: 먹으러 가다, 사러 오다.
patterns.exp: Use this to say you go or come somewhere for a purpose. It only works with movement verbs like 가다 (go), 오다 (come), 다니다 (attend). The -(으)러 part states the goal.
ex: I'm going to a restaurant to eat.
I came to meet a friend.

patterns.form: Add -으니까 after a consonant, -니까 after a vowel: 있다 → 있으니까.
patterns.exp: This is another way to say 'because', but it's the natural choice when the second clause is a suggestion, a request, or a command. Compare: use -아서/어서 for plain reasons, and -(으)니까 when you're telling someone to do something.
ex: It's dangerous, so please be careful.
It's raining, so take an umbrella.

patterns.form: Verbs take -는데; adjectives take -(으)ㄴ데 (좋다 → 좋은데). Past tense: -았/었는데.
patterns.exp: This is a very common connector that sets the scene for what comes next. It can mean 'and', a gentle 'but', or just background information ('I was doing X, when Y'). The exact shade depends on context, so read the whole sentence.
ex: I was eating when the phone rang.
This restaurant is good, but a bit expensive.

patterns.form: Add -으면서 after a consonant, -면서 after a vowel: 듣다 → 들으면서.
patterns.exp: Use this when one person does two things at the same time. Both actions must have the same subject.
ex: I study while listening to music.

patterns.form: Attach -거나 to each verb stem: 자거나 읽거나.
patterns.exp: Use -거나 to give a choice between two or more actions ('do A or B'). For choosing between nouns, use -(이)나 instead.
ex: On weekends I sleep or read.
I take the bus or walk.

patterns.form: Add -으려고 after a consonant, -려고 after a vowel: 사려고, 먹으려고.
patterns.exp: This states the aim behind an action ('in order to ~'). Unlike -(으)러, it works with any verb, not just movement verbs. It's often followed by what you did to achieve that aim.
ex: I stood in line to buy a ticket.
I exercise every day to lose weight.

patterns.form: Add -은 (consonant) or -ㄴ (vowel) to a verb stem, then the noun: 산 책, 먹은 밥.
patterns.exp: Korean puts descriptions before the noun, like a mini-sentence. This form describes a noun using a past action — 'the ~ that (someone) did'. English uses a relative clause ('the book I bought').
ex: The book I bought yesterday is interesting.
It's the food that I made.

patterns.form: Add -는 to a verb stem, then the noun: 읽는 책, 먹는 사람.
patterns.exp: This describes a noun using an action happening now or in general — 'the ~ that (someone) is doing / does'. Only action verbs use -는; descriptive verbs (adjectives) use -(으)ㄴ instead.
ex: The book I'm reading now is difficult.
The person coming over there is my friend.

patterns.form: Add -을 (consonant) or -ㄹ (vowel) to a verb stem, then the noun: 만날 사람, 먹을 것.
patterns.exp: This describes a noun using something not yet done — 'the ~ that (someone) will do / to do'. It's very common in phrases like 할 일 (things to do) and 먹을 것 (something to eat).
ex: Who is the person you'll meet tomorrow?
Please give me something to eat.

patterns.form: Add -기 to the verb/adjective stem: 수영하다 → 수영하기.
patterns.exp: This turns an action into a noun, like adding '-ing' or 'to ~' in English. It's used with many set expressions (-기 쉽다 'easy to', -기 시작하다 'begin to') and to talk about activities.
ex: Swimming is harder than I thought.
Getting up early in the morning is hard for me.

patterns.form: Add -음 after a consonant, -ㅁ after a vowel: 있다 → 있음, 크다 → 큼.
patterns.exp: This also turns a verb or adjective into a noun, but it's the formal, written-style version. You'll see it in notices, reports, and note-taking to state a fact concisely.
ex: We inform you that the meeting has been cancelled.
The museum is closed on Mondays. (notice style)

patterns.form: Add -는 것 to a verb stem; it can shorten to -는 게 (subject) or -는 걸 (object) in speech.
patterns.exp: This turns a whole action into a noun phrase so you can talk about it — 'reading', 'the fact that ~'. It's extremely common, especially with 좋아하다/싫어하다 (like/dislike doing something).
ex: Exercising every day is good for your health.

patterns.form: Add -기 전에 to the verb stem: 자다 → 자기 전에. With a noun, use 전에 (식사 전에).
patterns.exp: This says one action happens before another. The verb before -기 전에 stays in dictionary form — you do not mark tense on it.
ex: I brush my teeth before sleeping.

patterns.form: Add -은 후에 (consonant) or -ㄴ 후에 (vowel): 먹은 후에. 후 can be replaced by 다음 or 뒤.
patterns.exp: This says one action happens after another finishes. The verb takes the past-modifier form -(으)ㄴ even though the whole sentence may be present or future.
ex: After eating, I take a walk.

patterns.form: Add -을 때 (consonant) or -ㄹ 때 (vowel): 먹을 때. For a past 'when', use -았/었을 때.
patterns.exp: This marks the time when something happens ('when ~'). Use -(으)ㄹ 때 for general or present time, and -았/었을 때 for a specific past moment.
ex: When I was young, I lived in the countryside.
Call me when you go home.

patterns.form: Add -는 동안 to a verb stem. With a noun, use 동안 (방학 동안).
patterns.exp: This describes a stretch of time during which something else happens. The two actions can have different subjects, unlike -(으)면서.
ex: It rained while I was sleeping.

patterns.form: Add -을 수 있다/없다 (consonant) or -ㄹ 수 있다/없다 (vowel): 갈 수 있어요, 먹을 수 없어요.
patterns.exp: This expresses ability or possibility. 있다 means you can, 없다 means you can't. It covers both 'I know how to' and 'it is possible to'.
ex: 저는 한국어를 조금 할 수 있어요.
I can speak a little Korean.

patterns.form: Same vowel rule as -아요/어요, plus 하다 (or 되다): 가야 해요, 먹어야 해요.
patterns.exp: This says something is necessary or required — you have to do it. 하다 and 되다 are interchangeable here.
ex: I have to go right now.
You must take the medicine every day.

patterns.form: Same vowel rule plus 되다: 앉아도 돼요, 먹어도 돼요.
patterns.exp: This grants or asks permission ('you may ~', 'is it okay to ~?'). Add a question mark and rising tone to ask for permission.

patterns.form: Attach -(으)면 to the stem, then 안 되다: 피우면 안 돼요.
patterns.exp: This is the opposite of -아도/어도 되다: it says something is forbidden or not allowed. Literally 'if you do ~, it won't do'.
ex: You must not sleep during class.

patterns.form: Attach -고 싶다 to the verb stem: 가고 싶어요.
patterns.exp: This expresses your own desire to do something. It's one of the most useful beginner patterns.
ex: I want to go home soon.
I want to eat Korean food.
patterns.note: When talking about someone else's wish, use -고 싶어 하다: 동생이 자고 싶어 해요.

patterns.form: Add -을/ㄹ 줄 알다 (know how) or 모르다 (don't know how): 수영할 줄 알아요.
patterns.exp: This is specifically about having (or not having) a learned skill — 'I know how to ~'. It's different from -(으)ㄹ 수 있다, which is about general possibility.
ex: I don't know how to drive.

patterns.form: Attach -기로 하다 to the verb stem; often used in the past: -기로 했어요.
patterns.exp: This expresses a decision or agreement to do something. It's frequently used in the past tense to report a decision you've already made.
ex: I decided to start exercising tomorrow.
I arranged to meet my friend in the evening.

patterns.form: Add -을까요? (consonant) or -ㄹ까요? (vowel): 갈까요?, 먹을까요?
patterns.exp: Use this to suggest doing something together ('shall we ~?') or to wonder aloud about something ('I wonder if ~'). With 'we', it invites the other person's opinion.
ex: I wonder if the weather will be nice tomorrow.

patterns.form: Add -읍시다 (consonant) or -ㅂ시다 (vowel): 시작합시다.
patterns.exp: This is a formal way to suggest doing something together ('let's ~'). It's common in announcements and among colleagues, but can sound too strong to elders, so be careful with who you use it on.
ex: Let's all cheer up / do our best.

patterns.form: Same vowel rule plus 보다: 먹어 보다, 가 보다.
patterns.exp: This means to try doing something, or to do it and see what happens. In the past tense (-아/어 봤다) it means 'I have (ever) done ~' — talking about experience.

patterns.form: Attach -느라고 to an action-verb stem: 하다 → 하느라고.
patterns.exp: This explains a negative result caused by being occupied with something else — 'I was busy doing X, so I couldn't do Y'. The result is usually something that failed, was missed, or made you tired.
ex: I couldn't do my homework because I was busy playing games.
I didn't hear it because I was answering the phone.
patterns.note: Both clauses must share the same subject, and you cannot put a past-tense marker on -느라고 itself.

patterns.form: Attach -는 바람에 to an action-verb stem: 오다 → 오는 바람에.
patterns.exp: This gives the cause of an unplanned, usually negative result. The first event is sudden or out of your control, and it messed something up.
ex: 갑자기 비가 오는 바람에 소풍이 취소됐어요.
The picnic was cancelled because it suddenly rained.
I was late because I overslept.

patterns.form: Attach -더라도 to the stem: 힘들다 → 힘들더라도.
patterns.exp: This is a strong 'even if'. It concedes an extreme or hypothetical situation and says the result still holds. It's stronger and more hypothetical than the everyday -아도/어도.
ex: No matter how hard it is, don't give up.
내일 눈이 오더라도 여행을 갈 거예요.
Even if it snows tomorrow, I'll still travel.

patterns.form: Add -을/ㄹ 뻔하다, almost always in the past: -(으)ㄹ 뻔했다.
patterns.exp: This describes something that very nearly happened but didn't — a close call. It's always about a narrowly avoided (usually bad) event.
ex: The road was slippery, so I almost fell.
There was traffic, so I almost missed the flight.

patterns.form: Attach -자마자 to the verb stem: 도착하다 → 도착하자마자.
patterns.exp: This says the second action happens immediately after the first, with no gap. 'The moment I did X, Y happened.'
ex: As soon as I got home, I fell asleep.
I called as soon as I heard the news.

patterns.form: With a verb: -는 대로 ('as soon as'). With a noun: 대로 ('according to').
patterns.exp: After a verb, this means 'right after ~ happens' and is common in promises ('I'll contact you as soon as it's done'). It can also mean 'in accordance with' something.
ex: I'll contact you as soon as the work is done.
I'll do it just as you said.

patterns.form: Attach -게 되다 to the verb stem: 살다 → 살게 되다.
patterns.exp: This shows a change that happened because of circumstances, not entirely by your own choice — 'things turned out so that ~'. It often softens a statement, making it sound less like a personal decision.
ex: Because of work, I ended up living in Korea.
I came to know that fact later.

patterns.form: Attach to an adjective stem with the vowel rule: 따뜻하다 → 따뜻해지다.
patterns.exp: Added to a descriptive verb (adjective), this shows a gradual change into a new state — 'to get / become ~'. It turns 'is warm' into 'becomes warm'.
ex: Now that it's spring, the weather has become warm.

patterns.form: Attach -기 위해(서) to a verb stem. With a noun, use 을/를 위해(서).
patterns.exp: This states a clear purpose or goal ('in order to ~'). It's more formal than -(으)려고 and common in writing.
ex: I walk every day in order to stay healthy.
I study hard in order to pass the exam.

patterns.form: Verbs take -는 편이다; adjectives take -(으)ㄴ 편이다: 잘 먹는 편이다, 큰 편이다.
patterns.exp: This softens a statement to 'rather / relatively ~' instead of a flat 'is'. Koreans use it a lot to avoid sounding too absolute — 'I'm on the ~ side'.
ex: 저는 매운 음식을 잘 먹는 편이에요.
I'm rather good at eating spicy food.

patterns.form: Attach -더니 to the stem; the subject is usually someone/something other than 'I'.
patterns.exp: This recalls something you personally noticed, then gives a related follow-up or result. Often it contrasts a past observation with the present ('it was ~ earlier, and now ~').
ex: It was cold earlier, but now it's warm.
My sibling studied hard, and then passed the exam.

patterns.form: Past stem + -더니: 일어나다 → 일어났더니.
patterns.exp: Use this when YOU did something and then discovered a result. The first clause is your own past action; the second is what you found or what happened next.
ex: When I woke up in the morning, it had snowed.
After I took the medicine, I felt a bit better.

patterns.form: Add -을수록 (consonant) or -ㄹ수록 (vowel). Often paired with -(으)면: 보면 볼수록.
patterns.exp: This shows that as one thing increases, another changes along with it — 'the more X, the more Y'. Repeating the verb with -(으)면 first adds emphasis.
ex: The more I listen to this song, the more I like it.
The cheaper it is, the better it sells.

patterns.form: Attach -는 셈이다 (verbs) or -(으)ㄴ 셈이다 (adjectives/past).
patterns.exp: This says that although it may not be literally true, the situation is basically equivalent to something — 'it's practically ~', 'it comes down to ~'.
ex: 이 정도면 거의 다 끝난 셈이에요.
At this point, it's practically all finished.
한 달에 한 번 보니까 거의 안 보는 셈이에요.
We meet once a month, so we practically never see each other.

patterns.form: Attach -기 마련이다 to the verb/adjective stem. -게 마련이다 is the same.
patterns.exp: This states a general truth that is bound to happen — 'that's just how it is', 'naturally ~'. It's used for things everyone accepts as inevitable.
ex: Everyone is bound to make mistakes.
If you use it for a long time, it's bound to break.

patterns.form: Verbs take -는 반면(에); adjectives take -(으)ㄴ 반면(에).
patterns.exp: This contrasts two opposite qualities or facts in one sentence — one side is like this, while the other side is like that. Common in comparison and opinion writing.
ex: The older brother is quiet, whereas the younger one is lively.
이 일은 돈은 많이 버는 반면에 너무 힘들어요.
This job pays a lot, but on the other hand it's very hard.

patterns.form: Verbs take -는 데다가; adjectives take -(으)ㄴ 데다가.
patterns.exp: This piles one thing on top of another in the same direction — the second point adds to and strengthens the first ('not only ~, and also ~').
ex: 비가 오는 데다가 바람도 세게 불어요.
On top of the rain, the wind is blowing hard too.
That shop is cheap, and on top of that, friendly.

patterns.form: Add -을/ㄹ 뿐만 아니라. With a noun, use 뿐만 아니라.
patterns.exp: This adds a second, often bigger point to the first — 'not only A, but also B'. It's a formal, common way to build up an argument.
ex: He is not only smart but also diligent.
이 도시는 경치가 좋을 뿐만 아니라 음식도 맛있어요.
This city not only has nice scenery but also delicious food.

patterns.form: Attach -곤 하다 to the verb stem; often past: -곤 했다.
patterns.exp: This describes a repeated past habit — something you often did back then. It highlights that the action happened again and again over a period.
ex: 어릴 때 이 공원에서 자주 놀곤 했어요.
When I was young, I used to play often in this park.
힘들 때면 그 노래를 듣곤 해요.
When times are hard, I tend to listen to that song.

patterns.form: Add -은/ㄴ 채(로) to a verb stem: 신다 → 신은 채로.
patterns.exp: This describes doing something while an earlier state is still in place, usually one that's a bit unusual — 'with the shoes still on', 'while the light was left on'.
ex: He came into the room with his shoes still on.
I fell asleep with the light left on.

patterns.form: Verbs take -는 한; some fixed forms use -(으)ㄴ 한 (아는 한, 특별한 일이 없는 한).
patterns.exp: This sets a condition or limit under which something holds true — 'as long as / so far as ~'. It's common in the fixed phrase 내가 아는 한 ('as far as I know').
ex: 제가 아는 한 그건 사실이 아니에요.
As far as I know, that's not true.
비가 오지 않는 한 행사는 진행됩니다.
As long as it doesn't rain, the event will go ahead.

patterns.form: Add -을/ㄹ 리가 없다 to the stem.
patterns.exp: This strongly denies a possibility — 'it can't possibly be that ~', 'there's no way ~'. You use it when you're confident something isn't true.
ex: 그가 그런 말을 했을 리가 없어요.
There's no way he said something like that.
이렇게 열심히 했는데 실패할 리가 없어요.
I worked this hard — there's no way I'll fail.

patterns.form: Add -기는커녕 to the verb stem. With a noun, use 은/는커녕.
patterns.exp: This dismisses the first idea and says the reality is even less than that — 'far from doing X, I couldn't even do the smaller Y'. It always sets up a stronger negative that follows.
ex: Far from resting, I didn't even have time to eat.
Far from being praised, I only got scolded.

patterns.form: Nominalize with -(으)ㅁ, then 에도 불구하고: 노력했음에도 불구하고.
patterns.exp: This is a formal 'despite'. It concedes a fact and says the result went against what that fact would suggest. You'll mostly see it in writing and formal speech.
ex: Despite being old, he is very healthy.

patterns.m: 'to the point / on the verge of ~'
patterns.form: Add -을/ㄹ 지경이다 to the stem.
patterns.exp: This emphasizes an extreme state you've almost reached — 'so much that I could almost ~'. It exaggerates for effect, usually about hardship.
ex: I'm so hungry I could collapse.
I have so much work I could go crazy.

patterns.form: Attach 으로 인해(서) after a consonant, 로 인해(서) after a vowel/ㄹ, to a noun.
patterns.exp: This is a formal way to state a cause, used with nouns — 'due to ~'. It appears constantly in news reports and official notices.
ex: The road was blocked due to a sudden accident.
The flight was cancelled due to the typhoon.

patterns.m: 'as much as / to the extent that ~'
patterns.form: Verbs take -는 만큼; adjectives/past take -(으)ㄴ 만큼. With a noun, use 만큼.
patterns.exp: This shows that the second thing matches the degree of the first — 'in proportion to ~', 'to the extent that ~'. Often used to say results match effort.
ex: Good results came out in proportion to the effort.
They say you see as much as you know.

patterns.form: Attach -되 directly to the verb stem: 하다 → 하되.
patterns.exp: This is a formal, written connector that allows something but adds a condition or contrast — 'you may do X, but ~'. It's concise and common in rules and instructions.
ex: 이 약을 드시되 하루에 세 번만 드세요.
You may take this medicine, but only three times a day.
You may use it, but please handle it carefully.

patterns.form: Attach -고자 하다 to the verb stem.
patterns.exp: This is a formal statement of intention or purpose — 'we intend to ~', 'I wish to ~'. You'll hear it in speeches, presentations, and official writing.
ex: 오늘은 이 문제를 함께 해결하고자 합니다.
Today we intend to solve this problem together.
We wish to recruit new members.

patterns.form: Add -을/ㄹ 따름이다 to the stem.
patterns.exp: This is a formal, humble way to say there's nothing more than this feeling or action — 'I can only ~', 'I am simply ~'. Common in polite thanks or apologies.
ex: I can only be grateful that you helped me.
I am simply sorry for falling short of your expectations.
